content.text: Unmounting is the final phase of a React component's life: it is removed from the tree and its resources must be released. The cleanup functions returned by
are the primary mechanism for this. Missing cleanup causes memory leaks, ghost event handlers, and stale state updates.

attrs.alt: Unmounting: cleanup sequence and resource leaks diagram

content.text: A conditional expression in the parent changes from rendering the component to not rendering it (
prop changes — React treats this as unmount + remount.
The application navigates to a route that does not include the component (in routing libraries).

A cleanup function is the return value of an effect's setup function. It runs before the component's DOM is removed and before the effect re-runs on the next update (for effects that depend on changing values).
The same cleanup function handles both update-driven re-runs and unmounting. React does not provide a separate "update cleanup" and "unmount cleanup" — the same function is called in both cases.

In StrictMode development, React mounts, unmounts, and remounts every component immediately after the initial mount. This means cleanup runs on the first mount before the component is even visible. If your cleanup function breaks the component when called after a fresh setup, the effect is not correctly written.
Any resource acquired during setup must be released during cleanup:
Fetch requests that complete after the component unmounts cause the "can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component" warning (in React 17 and earlier). Cancel them with AbortController:
AbortController cancels the network request at the browser level, not just the state update. This frees bandwidth and avoids processing the response for a component that no longer exists.
must be the exact same function reference that was passed to

. If you define the handler inside the effect (as above), the closure captures it, and the cleanup removes it correctly. Defining the handler outside the effect and referencing it in both calls also works.

The most common cause of memory leaks in React applications is forgetting to unsubscribe from event emitters, stores, or observables in the cleanup function. The subscriber holds a reference to the component's closure, which holds references to React state, props, and the component itself — preventing garbage collection.
Asserting that cleanup was called confirms the component does not leave resources running after it is removed — a class of bugs that is otherwise difficult to catch before production.

Unmounting is triggered by conditional rendering, key changes, and parent unmounts. Cleanup functions — the return values of effect setup functions — run before the DOM is removed and before an effect re-runs. Every resource acquired in an effect (timers, event listeners, subscriptions, observers, fetch requests) must have a corresponding cleanup. Missing cleanup causes memory leaks, ghost handlers, and stale state updates. StrictMode validates cleanup by running unmount immediately after the initial mount.
When integrating third-party libraries that manage their own DOM (charts, maps, editors, sliders), the cleanup function is where you call the library's destroy or dispose method.
call, a new chart instance is created on every re-render while the old one still holds its canvas reference, causing a "Canvas is already in use" error.

A common requirement is running an exit animation before the component is removed from the DOM. React does not have built-in deferred unmounting, but libraries like Framer Motion and React Transition Group handle this by keeping the component mounted briefly after a conditional rendering toggle, then removing it once the animation completes.
When a component suspends (throws a Promise, typically via

or a Suspense-compatible data fetching library), React unmounts the suspended subtree and mounts the fallback. Effect cleanup runs when the component suspends, just as it would on a normal unmount. When the data resolves, the component remounts and effects run again.

This means that if your app uses Suspense, your effects may be mounted and unmounted multiple times as data loads. Ensure cleanup is symmetric with setup so repeated mount/unmount cycles do not accumulate side effects.

React portals render children into a DOM node outside the parent component's DOM hierarchy. Cleanup for portals follows the same rules — effects inside a portal run on the portal's mount/unmount, which is tied to the host component's lifecycle.

The portal's effects unmount when the parent component that renders the portal unmounts, not when the portal's DOM container is removed. Clean up resources in the portal's effects just as you would in a regular component.
"Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component" (React 17 and earlier) indicates a missing fetch cancellation or timer cleanup.
Open DevTools → Memory → take heap snapshots before and after mounting/unmounting a component. If the component's closure or subscriptions still appear in the snapshot, cleanup is missing.
StrictMode exposes cleanup bugs by running setup → cleanup → setup. If your component breaks on the second setup, the first cleanup did not release the resource fully.
event listener and the handler fires multiple times per event, the listener is accumulating because each mount adds one without removal.
inside the cleanup return function. If it never prints during unmount, the effect either has no cleanup or the component is not being unmounted when expected.

for subscriptions or timers. Confirm every resource has a matching cleanup, and write a test that calls
